deck.title: Talksmith Style Reference
deck.class: One example of every slide template, plus layout edge cases

slides.title: This deck renders one example of every Talksmith slide template.
slides.sub: Flip through it, or diff style-reference.html, and any styling regression jumps out.

slides.title: A divider is a plain sub-opener within a section
A statement slide is one dominant claim, set large.
slides.sub: No bullets, no cards — just the single line you want the room to remember.

slides.title: A statement title can run long, and it should still wrap gracefully across two or three lines while reading as one strong, uninterrupted assertion.
big-number: one hero metric
slides.caption: big-number is for a single hero figure — one number set very large, one caption, nothing competing with it.

slides.quote: A quote slide is the whole slide — one voiced line, centered, with an attribution beneath.

callout.label: Think of it this way
callout.body: A callout is a single rounded panel for a tip, analogy, or warning — pink for a lateral note, blue for a declarative takeaway.

slides.title: single-point: one lead plus one point

point.label: The rule
point.body: When a slide is a claim plus exactly one supporting beat, give that beat a card — never a lone bullet floating under a title.

cards.label: Two is valid
cards.body: A two-item labeled set is a real concept-breakdown, not something to drop to prose.

cards.label: Iconed
cards.body: Each card still gets its own distinct, content-matched icon above the label.

cards.label: Unordered
cards.body: The cards have no sequence; order carries no meaning here.

cards.label: Short bodies
cards.body: Keep each body to a line or two so the grid stays even.

cards.label: No source image
cards.body: Any picture would route the slide to figures instead.

slides.title: concept-breakdown: 4 cards (2×2), longer bodies edge case

cards.label: Capacity
cards.body: Two through six cards fit; beyond six, split across two slides so nothing has to shrink.

cards.label: Wrapping
cards.body: Longer bodies wrap inside their card, and all four cards hold the same height as a 2×2 grid.

cards.label: Distinct icons
cards.body: The renderer never repeats an icon within a slide, so four cards means four different glyphs.

cards.label: When to switch
cards.body: If a body is too long to read comfortably here, move it to icon-list, which gives each row prose room.

cards.label: Default home
cards.body: The general case for any labeled set of two or more.

cards.label: Grid
cards.body: Six cards lay out as a 3×2 grid with uniform gutters.

cards.label: Icon each
cards.body: Six distinct icons, one anchoring every concept.

cards.label: Terse
cards.body: At six cards, keep every body to a single short line.

cards.label: Split point
cards.body: Need more than six? Split the set across two slides.

cards.label: Still cards
cards.body: Even this dense, it is cards — never a bullet list.

slides.title: aside: a full-bleed image column on the right
slides.lead: The image reinforces the point; the text carries it.

rows.label: Atmosphere, not information
rows.body: The column crops to fill, so nothing in it needs to be read closely.

rows.label: Default side
rows.body: An aside sits on the right unless the hint says left.

rows.label: Any stage-based type
rows.body: The stage macro adds it, so every content template supports it.

image.alt: An abstract texture standing in for an atmospheric photo

slides.notes: The aside is the slide-model expression of an author's `<!-- aside: ... -->` hint in draft.md. It reserves roughly 37% of the slide width as a full-bleed image column and pads the stage on that side so the head and body lay out in what remains. Because the column crops to fill rather than fitting the whole image, it is only ever atmosphere: a diagram, chart, or screenshot the audience must actually read belongs in a template that owns its image, where it is contained and legible.

slides.title: aside: the same column, on the left

cards.label: side: left
cards.body: The hint's optional side moves the column to the other edge.

cards.label: Content reflows
cards.body: The stage pads whichever side the image takes.

image.alt: An abstract texture standing in for an atmospheric photo

slides.notes: The left variant exists for rhythm: alternating the side across a run of slides keeps a deck from feeling like a template. The choice carries no semantics — it is purely visual pacing.

slides.title: card-row: 3 short cards with a lead
slides.lead: A lead line sits above a single horizontal row of three to five short cards:

cards.label: Lead
cards.body: One framing sentence introduces the row.

cards.label: Short
cards.body: Each body stays under about eighty characters.

cards.label: One row
cards.body: Three to five cards share one horizontal band.

slides.title: icon-list: rows with room for prose
slides.lead: Pick icon-list over card-row when at least one item needs two to four sentences:

rows.label: Vertical stack
rows.body: The rows stack top to bottom instead of sitting in one horizontal band, which gives every item room for a longer explanation without shrinking the type.

rows.label: One icon per row
rows.body: A line-art icon sits at the left of each row, content-matched and never repeated within the slide.

rows.label: Three to five rows
rows.body: Three rows are comfortable with full sentences; five rows only when the bodies are short.

slides.title: icon-list also holds a bare anaphora

rows.label: Each parallel line becomes one row.
This keeps such slides off the fallback path.

slides.title: process: an ordered sequence of steps
slides.lead: Use process when order carries meaning — steps, phases, or a numbered flow:

steps.label: Classify
steps.body: The fill step reads final.md and picks each slide's template.

steps.label: Decompose
steps.body: It splits the body into that template's exact required fields.

steps.label: Render
steps.body: build_html.py maps those fields onto Jinja templates, mechanically.

slides.title: process: plain numbered steps
slides.lead: When the steps are ordered but unlabeled, render them as a vertical numbered list:

steps.body: Identify the data you are about to share and where it came from.
Classify its sensitivity before it leaves your workspace.
Choose an approved tool for that classification.
Record what was shared so the workflow can be audited later.

slides.title: timeline: a dated milestone sequence

milestones.label: Day 1
milestones.body: Reach for timeline when the labels read as dates or milestones.

milestones.label: Day 2
milestones.body: Time flows top to bottom along a connecting rail with a dot per entry.

milestones.label: Day 3
milestones.body: It differs from process only in that here, when matters more than the step.

slides.lead: An optional lead can frame the sequence before the dates begin.

cells: A comparison needs a real HTML table
The two columns should look different
It renders as a card-per-row grid
Both columns share the same weight and width

slides.title: pros-cons: upside versus downside
pros: The pro column uses the blue tint with a check
Best for a decision framed as a trade-off
Each line is one short point
cons: The con column uses the pink tint with a mark
Not for a general A-versus-B comparison

slides.title: stat: two to four standalone numbers
slides.lead: Each number is a value with a short caption beneath it:

stats.caption: the number of metrics a stat slide holds
caption line per value, baselines aligned across the row
bullets — the numbers themselves are the payload

highlights.body: A hero single metric goes on big-number instead.

slides.title: content-image: text beside an image
slides.lead: Each fact leads with a bold label the renderer bolds for you:

facts.label: One image
facts.body: supports one to three short facts.

facts.label: Aspect preserved
facts.body: the image is never cropped or forced into a box.

highlights.label: Never cropped
highlights.body: the tall portrait must show its full height, edge to edge.

slides.title: content-image: image-top layout for short text
facts: When the text is just a line, the image-top layout lets the image dominate the slide.

slides.title: figures: a set where each item has its own image
slides.lead: Three or more figure cards, each an image with a label and a line:

figures.label: Image per item
figures.body: This is what separates figures from concept-breakdown.

figures.label: Uniform crop
figures.body: Every figure shares the same size and alignment.

figures.label: Label and line
figures.body: A short caption sits under each image.

slides.title: image-grid: four or more images as one composite

cards.label: Cards on one side
cards.body: A labeled set fills one half of the slide.

cards.label: Image on the other
cards.body: A single supporting visual fills the rest.

slides.title: code-example: a readable code surface
explanation: The code sits on a monospace surface with syntax colors.
A short explanation column reads alongside it.

slides.title: content-text: the prose last resort
slides.big: content-text carries only prose — a definition or a framing — when nothing richer fits.
panels: It renders as one lead line plus a few light panels, never a paragraph and never bullets.
The fill flags it as a restructure candidate: most prose slides are really cards in disguise.

slides.title: Highlights: the six kinds
slides.big: Any content slide can carry a highlights band; the fill picks each line's kind:

highlights.label: Takeaway
highlights.body: the one idea to remember (the default kind).

highlights.label: Important
highlights.body: a risk, a limit, or a hard 'do not'.

slides.title: Quiz
slides.question: In a Talksmith quiz slide, when does the answer appear?
slides.answer: On next-nav — the question shows first so the audience can think, then the answer fades up.
slides.explanation: The correct choice highlights in sync; space is reserved so the question never jumps.
slides.notes: Let the room commit to an answer before you advance; the reveal is one navigation step away.

slides.title: Questions?
slides.body: Thanks for flipping through the Talksmith template catalog.
